headline: CATTLE SURGE 4.2% IGNITES BROAD RALLY
subheadline: Grains climb with live cattle while precious metals spike on broader risk appetite.
lead: Live cattle exploded 4.2% higher to $243.30, leading a broad commodity rally that pulled grains and oils along for the ride. Soybeans gained 12½¢ to $11.72 while corn added 2½¢ to $4.58, but the real story is growing protein demand meeting tight cattle supplies. With April planting season opening, input costs remain manageable despite energy's mixed signals.
teaser: Cattle surge ignites commodity rally as planting season begins.

one_number.context: This 4.2% surge reflects the tightest cattle inventory in decades meeting robust protein demand. For integrated operations, this creates a profitable hedge opportunity — high cattle prices can offset higher corn feeding costs while locking in margins before summer weather risks emerge.

sections.title: GRAINS & OILSEEDS
sections.body: Soybeans led grains higher, jumping 12½¢ to $11.72 on broad commodity strength and technical buying. Corn followed with a modest 2½¢ gain to $4.58, while Chicago wheat added 8¼¢ to $6.18. The rally feels more about risk appetite returning to commodities than fundamental shifts, but new-crop soybeans at $11.58 are testing resistance levels that matter for fall pricing decisions. Oats climbed 5¼¢ to $3.53, benefiting from the rising tide.
sections.bottom_line: Grains caught the commodity wave but fundamentals haven't changed overnight.
sections.farmer_action: Hold current positions — this looks like technical buying, not demand-driven strength.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK & DAIRY
sections.body: Live cattle's 4.2% explosion to $243.30 was the day's defining move, driven by tight supplies and strong protein demand fundamentals. Feeder cattle joined the party with a 1.3% gain to $366.42, while hogs bucked the trend, falling 1.0% to $104.85 on seasonal pressure. Class III milk barely budged at $17.38, up just 0.1%. The cattle move creates interesting dynamics for integrated operations — higher protein prices can justify higher feed costs.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle strength dominates, but hogs remain under seasonal pressure.
sections.farmer_action: Consider forward-selling cattle on this rally — margins are attractive and volatility is high.

sections.title: ENERGY & INPUTS
sections.body: Energy markets split personality: crude oil slipped 0.7% to $102.30 while natural gas jumped 1.4% to $2.88. With natgas still sitting just 5% off its 52-week low, fertilizer economics remain favorable for spring application decisions. The crude decline helps diesel costs, but at $102.30, fuel remains a meaningful planting expense. Spring application window is narrowing — ground needs to dry but prices could move.
sections.bottom_line: Mixed energy signals, but input costs remain manageable for planting season.
sections.farmer_action: Lock in fuel now if you haven't — crude at $102 is workable but vulnerable to geopolitical spikes.

sections.title: MACRO & TRADE
sections.body: Precious metals led the macro rally with silver spiking 4.8% to $75.67 and gold surging 2.8% to $4,712, signaling inflation hedging demand is back. The S&P 500 jumped 2.9% to $6,528, while the dollar held steady at 100.00. Risk appetite returning to commodities creates tailwinds for ag prices, but also signals potential inflation pressures ahead. The metal moves suggest investors are positioning for currency debasement concerns.
sections.bottom_line: Broad risk-on rally benefits commodities but signals inflation concerns brewing.
sections.farmer_action: Monitor currency moves — dollar weakness could extend grain rally beyond fundamentals.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Cattle Moves Matter for Grain Farmers
the_more_you_know.body: Live cattle prices directly impact corn demand — every dollar increase in cattle prices typically supports corn by 3-5¢ through increased feeding profitability. When cattle surge like today's 4.2% move, feedlots can afford higher corn prices, creating a floor under grain markets. This relationship works both ways: expensive corn can pressure cattle margins, while cheap grain enables cattle feeding expansion. For mixed operations, this creates natural hedging opportunities.

watch_list.desc: Weekly cattle on feed report — watch for placement numbers that could extend today's rally.
Weather models for next week's planting conditions across Corn Belt.
USDA planting intentions report — first official acreage guidance for 2026 crop.

daily_quote.text: Lock in fuel when it's cheap. Lock in grain prices when they're profitable. Both are perishable opportunities.

source_summary: CME Group · USDA · EIA · Federal Reserve
